coredata.dc:title: A 3D hydrogel based on chitosan and carbon dots for sensitive fluorescence detection of microRNA-21 in breast cancer cells
coredata.dc:creator: Mohammadi, Susan
Mohammadi, Somayeh
Salimi, Abdollah
coredata.dc:description: Hydrogels are 3D polymeric networks with great swelling capability in water and appropriate chemical, mechanical and biological features which make it feasible to maintain bioactive substances. Herein, we fabricated carbon dots-chitosan nanocomposite hydrogels via reacting carbon dots synthesized from various aldehyde precursors with chitosan after that functionalized with ssDNA probe for detection of microRNA-21 in MCF-7 cancer cells. More importantly, three fluorescent hydrogels were produced using schiff base reaction (forming imine bonds) among the amine in chitosan and aldehyde groups on the CDs surface. Furthermore, the hydrogel films, CDs and CDs-chitosan nanocomposite hydrogels were characterized by UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra, FT-IR, scanning electron microscope (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The DNA hydrogel bioassay strategy revealed a great stability and a superb sensitivity for microRNA-21, with a suitable linear range (0.1–125 fM) and a detection limit (0.03 fM). For sample analysis, the biosensors exhibited good linearity with MCF-7 cancer cell concentrations from 1000 to 25000, 1000–25000 and 1000–6000 cells mL−1 and detection limit of 310, 364 and 552 cells mL−1, for glutaraldehyde, nitrobezaldehyde and benzaldehyde based nanocomposite hydrogels, respectively. In addition, cell viability consequences demonstrated low probe cytotoxicity, so nanocomposite hydrogels was utilized to multicolor imaging of MCF-7 cancer cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: DNA hydrogel Biosensing
Turn off fluorescence sensor
MicroRNA-21
Carbon dots
MCF-7 cancer cells

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of biosensor based on hydrogel-CDs composite
Characterization of CDs and CDs-chitosan nanocomposite hydrogels
Mechanism of fluorescence sensing of microRNA-21 with nanocomposite hydrogels
Optimization of the experimental conditions for microRNA-21 detection
Analytical efficiency and specificity of the biosensors
Imaging of miR-21 in MCF-7 cells

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Research Office of University of Kurdistan

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge Iran National Science Foundation (INSF) ( grant number 96002684 ) Research Office of University of Kurdistan (grant number 4.1404086 ) for the financial support. The Iranian Nanotechnology Initiative support is also acknowledged.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–vis, FTIR, TEM and SEM characterization of three hydrogels and CDs: (a) UV–vis spectra, (b)FTIR spectra and (c) SEM image of glutaraldehyde synthesized hydrogels; (d) TEM of glutaraldehyde synthesized CDs; (e) UV–vis spectra, (f) FTIR spectra and (g) SEM image of nitrobenzaldehyde synthesized hydrogels; (h) TEM of nitrobenzaldehyde synthesized CDs; (i) UV–vis spectra, (j) FTIR spectra and (k) SEM image of benzaldehyde synthesized hydrogels; (l) SEM of the carbon dot-free hydrogel.

Emission spectra of the GA-CDs-CH (a), NB-CDs-CH (b) and B-CDs-CH (c) nanocomposite hydrogel, respectively, at different excitation wavelengths as displayed. The effect of DNA probe concentration to modification of GA-CDs-CH (d), NB-CDs-CH (e) and B-CDs-CH (f) nanocomposite hydrogel, respectively.

Fluorescence spectra of the three nanocomposite hydrogels with the addition of various concentrations of target microRNA-21 at 0.1–125 fM (0.1, 0.3, 0.7, 1, 1.5, 4.5, 8, 16.4, 26.3, 125 fM), (A–C) the corresponding calibration graph and the linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity of nanocomposite hydrogels (ΔF(F0–F)) and the various concentration of microRNA-21 for GA-CDs-CH (a), NB-CDs-CH (b) and B-CDs-CH (c) nanocomposite hydrogel, respectively. (d–f) Fluorescence intensity and calibration curves of CDs-CH nanocomposite hydrogels in the presence of MCF-7 cancer cells at range 1000–25,000 cells mL−1, logarithmic and linear relationship between ΔF (F0–F) and the different cell line numbers (1000, 2000, 3000, 6000, 10,000, 20,000 and 25,000 cells mL−1) for GA-CDs-CH (d), NB-CDs-CH (e) and B-CDs-CH (f) nanocomposite hydrogel, respectively.
Effect of interfering species for GA-CDs-CH (a), NB-CDs-CH (b) and B-CDs-CH (c) nanocomposite hydrogels.

Confocal laser scanning microscopy images of MCF-7 cells: (a–f) control, (g–l) MCF-7 cells prepared with 0.4 mg mL−1 Nitrobenzaldehyde carbon dots, (a, g) excited with a bright field, (b, h) Blue, (c, i) green, (d, j) yellow, (e, k) red and (f, l) merge. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Synthesis of the CDs and hydrogel preparation process. (a) Synthesis of CDs aliphatic dialdehyde (glutaraldehyde, (I)), Aromatic aldehyde with nitro functional group (electron-withdrawing group) (nitrobenzaldehyde, (II)) and an aromatic aldehyde (benzaldehyde, (III)). (b) The schematic for fabricating the fluorescent CDs-chitosan-based hydrogel for micRNA-21 detection; insertion of CDs and probe DNA into the gel network by Schiff base reaction (imine bond formation) between aldehyde functional groups on the surface of CDs and amine groups in the chitosan hydrogel framework and probe DNA are shown (CDs-CH Hydrogel-probe DNA).
Comparison of the analytical performance of suggested assay with different previously published techniques for determination of microRNA.

row.entry:
0.1 to 125 fM for GA-CDs-CH 0.1 to 125 fM for NB-CDs-CH hydrogel
0.1 to 26.3 fM for B-CDs-CH hydrogel

head.ce:title: A 3D hydrogel based on chitosan and carbon dots for sensitive fluorescence detection of microRNA-21 in breast cancer cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, University of Kurdistan, 66177-15175, Sanandaj, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, University of Kurdistan, 66177-15175, Sanandaj, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Center for Nanotechnology, University of Kurdistan, 66177-15175, Sanandaj, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Center for Nanotechnology, University of Kurdistan, 66177-15175, Sanandaj, Iran

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Chemistry, University of Kurdistan, 66177-15175, Sanandaj, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hydrogels are 3D polymeric networks with great swelling capability in water and appropriate chemical, mechanical and biological features which make it feasible to maintain bioactive substances. Herein, we fabricated carbon dots-chitosan nanocomposite hydrogels via reacting carbon dots synthesized from various aldehyde precursors with chitosan after that functionalized with ssDNA probe for detection of microRNA-21 in MCF-7 cancer cells. More importantly, three fluorescent hydrogels were produced using schiff base reaction (forming imine bonds) among the amine in chitosan and aldehyde groups on the CDs surface. Furthermore, the hydrogel films, CDs and CDs-chitosan nanocomposite hydrogels were characterized by UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra, FT-IR, scanning electron microscope (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The DNA hydrogel bioassay strategy revealed a great stability and a superb sensitivity for microRNA-21, with a suitable linear range (0.1–125 fM) and a detection limit (0.03 fM). For sample analysis, the biosensors exhibited good linearity with MCF-7 cancer cell concentrations from 1000 to 25000, 1000–25000 and 1000–6000 cells mL−1 and detection limit of 310, 364 and 552 cells mL−1, for glutaraldehyde, nitrobezaldehyde and benzaldehyde based nanocomposite hydrogels, respectively. In addition, cell viability consequences demonstrated low probe cytotoxicity, so nanocomposite hydrogels was utilized to multicolor imaging of MCF-7 cancer cells.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogels are 3D polymeric networks with high swelling capacity in water and appropriate chemical, mechanical and biological features which make it feasible to maintain bioactive substances [1–4]. Thanks for outstanding features, hydrogels have been considerably studied in various arenas including drug release [5], tissue engineering [6], and biosensors [3,7]. The conventional hydrogels were prepared using synthetic polymers including polyacrylamide [8,9], poly (N-isopropyl-acrylamide) (PNIPAM) [10], and Poly(vinyl alcohol) [11]. However, natural polymers such as chitosan [12], cellulose [13], and alginate [14] are beneficial in their good renewability and biocompatibility, and therefore they have been considered as the promising candidates for making great efficiency hydrogel materials. Numerous nanomaterials, such as carbon-based [3,14], magnetic [15], silica [16], and metal-based nanoparticles [17] have been incorporated into a hydrogel network for improving mechanical and optical features [18]. Carbon dots (CDs) are considered as a newest member in carbon nanomaterials family with size <10 nm. Depending on the primary materials used to synthesize CDs, different functional groups can be placed on their surface and used to modify its surface for different applications. By controlling the ratio of raw materials and other synthesis parameters, the emission wavelength and the fluorescence intensity of the synthesized CDs are changeable. Also, CDs have multiple benefits, such as the large surface area, good conductivity, fast charge transfer, spherical shape, various surface functional groups, possible heteroatom doping, low toxicity, water solubility, biocompatiblity, low production cost, simple synthesis methods, and photo stability. Therefore, due to the significant advantages of CDs they are a better alternative to other expensive nanomaterials, such as carbon dots and silver nanoclusters [19–22]. The significant advantages of CDs are utilized for various bio imaging [23], drug delivery [24,25], biological labeling [26,27], light emitting diodes (LEDs) [28], storage applications [29], and fluorescent sensing [30]. CDs have exploited as guest species in ionogels [31], hydrogels [32], and organogels [33], and hybrids of materials for practical applications. The CDs serve as both fluorophores and covalent cross-linkers in the gel framework [34]. Due to the dispersion of CDs, composite hydrogels exhibited good fluorescent features [3]. Stabilizing the CDs in the hydrogel system, not only does modulates the physicochemical features of the hydrogels but it also prevents the aggregation of nanoparticles and quenching of their fluorescence [34].

MicroRNAs are a sequence of non-coding small RNA molecules about 22 nucleotides in length and are effective in the regulation of gene expression. MicroRNAs inappropriate expression acts a role in the prognosis and incidence of all types of cancer. Therefore, microRNAs as biomarkers are of great significance in the diagnosis of cancer. Since there is a clearly recognizable relationship between enhanced microRNA-21 expression and breast cancer, microRNA-21 is a biomarker for early-stage breast cancer detection. Traditional procedures for microRNAs quantification have limitations such as: high cost, time consuming, low sensitivity. Thus, the development of rapid, selective, high sensitive, efficient and cost effective diagnostic methods are an urgent requirement for identification of biomarkers for early diagnosis of cancer [35–40]. Owing to the restrictions of conventional approaches, other strategies such as sensors and biosensors, including colorimetric [41], electrochemical [42], surface plasmon resonance [43], surface-enhanced raman spectroscopy (SERS) [44], electrochemiluminescence [45], and fluorescence [46] have been constructed to measure microRNAs. Among these techniques, fluorescence biosensors are more widely utilized to detect microRNAs because of its benefits including low cost, have a fast response time, easy operation and appropriate for biomedical applications [35].

In this study, we have successfully designed and fabricated a fluorescence-based biosensor using hydrogel nanocomposites as fluorophores, through combining chitosan and CDs synthesized from different aldehydes in a single system. With a logical design, such hydrogels can be simply synthesized in aqueous solution by forming an imine bond between chitosan and aldehyde functional groups of the CDs surface. Specifically, chitosan bearing –NH2/−OH groups, CDs bearing –COH groups, and ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) molecules carrying –COOH groups can form associated chitosan−CDs−EDTA complexes via hydrogen-bonding interactions. The fluorescent hydrogel composite was functionalized by DNA probe and then utilized as a detecting platform for rapid and selective quantification of microRNA-21.

Nitrobenzaldehyde, benzaldehyde and chitosan were purchased from Merck Company. Glutaraldehyde (GA, 25%) and dialysis bag (molecular weight cut off = 1000 Da) were bought from Sigma-Aldrich. All oligonucleotide sequences were produced by Takapouzist Company. The utilized sequences are as follows:
MicroRNA-21(target): UAGCUUAUCAGACUGAUGUUGA.
DNA probe, [C6 Amine] TCAACATCAGTCTGATAAGCT;
One-base mismatched microRNA-21: -UAGCUUAUCACACUGAUGUUGA;

All of the fluorescence spectra were measured using LS 55 Perkin Elmer spectrophotometer (Germany). The Ultraviolet–Visible (UV–Vis) spectrophotometer, SPECTROD 250 Analytik Jena model from the USA was used to record absorption spectra. The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) analyses were carried out using a Bruker-Vector 22 spectrometer (Germany). SEM images were recorded using a Field Emission-Scanning Electron Microscopy (FE-SEM), TSCAN. TEM images of synthesized nanomaterials were recorded using H600 TEM (Hitachi, Japan). The cell images were recorded using confocal laser scanning microscopy (Nikon Elipse Ti, Netherlands).

The CDs preparation process is illustrated in Scheme 1 (A) . Three types of aldehyde-CDs including glutaraldehyde, nitrobenzaldehyde and benzaldehyde were synthesized by the solvothermal method. Two CDs types of glutaraldehyde and benzaldehyde were prepared using previous reported method with minor modification [34].

Firstly, 300 μL of glutaraldehyde was mixed with 600 μL of ethanol. The mixture transferred to a poly (tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave (20 mL), and heated in the oven at 160 °C for 2 h [34], resulted in a brown colloid solution, afterwards centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 10 min to remove high-weight carbon aggregates. The supernatant is embedded in the dialysis tubing for 12 h and the dialyzed solution has kept in the refrigerator.

For the synthesis of nitro benzaldehyde based CDs, 0.002 g of 4-nitro benzaldehyde was mixed with 5 mL of ethanol and heated by solvothermal treatment at 180 °C for 15 h. A pale brown solution indicated that the CDs was synthesized. The CDs suspensions was centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 15 min. Then added to a dialysis membrane for 6 h and resulted solution has stored in the refrigerator at 4 °C.

For the synthesis of benzaldehyde based CDs, 300 μL of benzaldehyde was added to 50 mL of ethanol in an autoclave glass container, and heated in the oven at 180 °C for 15 h [34], and the obtained suspension was centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 15 min. Then, the yellow CDs solution was utilized for further experiments.

The hydrogel preparation process is illustrated in Scheme 1 (B). In the first step: 300 μL of each CDs was dried (GA-CDs: 0.2 g, NB-CDs: 0.1 g and B-CDs 0.03 g). In the second stage: Chitosan-based hydrogels were prepared as described below. Initially, 0.1 g of chitosan and 20 mL of distilled water were mixed and heated to 70 °C, and then 100 μL of acetic acid (0.5%) was added to mixture at the same temperature. Then the dried CDs was dissolved in 8 mL of the chitosan solution through stirring at the same temperature for 30 min. 0.012 g of EDTA was added to the solution and stirred for 60 min 80 μL of ethanol and 72 μL of glutaraldehyde were added drop by drop as a cross-linker under stirring at the same temperature conditions. The pH of the resulting suspension was 3.46, which was gradually neutralized by the addition of NaOH to pH = 7 and CDs-chitosan hydrogel was slowly formed. Chitosan plays a significant role in the preparation of three-dimensional hydrogel frames. CDs act as both cross-linking agents and fluorescent donors in this assay [3].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of biosensor based on hydrogel-CDs composite
ce:section.ce:para: Initially, 1 mL of PBS (pH = 7) was added to 0.0364 g of the prepared hydrogel and sonicated until the hydrogel was completely dispersed. Then 20 μL of the amine modified DNA probe (100 nM) is added to the solution and kept in the refrigerator for 24 h to modify CDs surface and used for subsequent experiments.

Firstly 300 μL of CDs-CH nanocomposite hydrogels (prepared in Section 2.4) were diluted with 200 μL of 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.4) and then sonicated, the fluorescence measurement was performed with excitation wavelength of 360 nm for NB-CD-CH and B-CD-CH and 440 nm for GA-CD-CH. After that, different concentrations of microR-21 (0.1–125 fM) were added to the mixture and the fluorescence intensities were measured.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CDs and CDs-chitosan nanocomposite hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: The optical features of both CDs and CDs-chitosan nanocomposite hydrogels evaluated by UV–vis absorption spectroscopy. The UV–vis absorption of prepared CDs shown in Fig. 1 (a, e, i) display that the UV absorption peaks of GA-CDs, NB-CDs, and B-CDs are at (235 nm and 310 nm), (264 nm and 300 nm), and (230 nm and 310 nm), respectively. A peak centered on around of 230 nm is ascribed to π-π* transition of sp2 carbons, while a shoulder at around 310 nm is attributed n-π* transition of the CO bonds [45]. Owing to the reaction between the CDs and the chitosan matrix, the peaks shifted to higher wavelengths (Fig. 1 a, orange spectrum), which is related to the formation of imine bond [34] that causes the CDs to immobilize in the hydrogel system. A red shift in the position of the bands of UV absorption spectra were also observed for NB-CDs-CH and B-CDs-CH hydrogels (Fig. 1e and i).

FT-IR spectroscopy experiments have been performed to show specific molecular transformations associated with hydrogel formation (Fig. 1b, f and j). According to FT-IR analysis for GA-CDs-CH hydrogel (blue spectrum in Fig. 1b), shows vibrational peaks at 2800-3300 cm−1 attributed to –OH, –NH2 and –CH stretching. The bands observed in the 1300 cm−1 and 1400 cm−1 regions correspond to the stretching vibrations of -C-O and -C-C alkanes, respectively. The bond in the 1600-1650 cm−1 region is related to imine (–CN) [34], which is attributed to the Schiff base reaction between –COH groups on the surface of CDs and major amines within the chitosan matrix, resulting in the formation of imine bond (blue spectrum in Fig. 1b). The NB-CDs and the NB-CDs-CH nanocomposite hydrogel also exhibit similar peaks. But one more peak was observed at 1380 cm−1 (for –NO) [48]. For B-CDs a few more peaks was observed at the ~1180-1050 cm−1 (for -C-O), ~1590 cm−1 (for –CC), and ~920 cm−1 (for –CC bending) [34]. Changes in the FT-IR spectrum were similarly observed after the formation of the NB-CDs-CH hydrogel and the B-CDs-CH hydrogel (Fig. 1 f and j).

TEM images from prepared aldehyde CDs were used to describe the size and morphology of nanoparticles. According to TEM analysis, the nanoparticles have a relatively uniform distribution with nearly spherical morphology. Also, the average particle sizes of CDs were 1 ± 0.7 nm, 5 ± 3.1 nm, for GA-CDs and NB-CDs, respectively (Fig. 1d and h). Analysis of SEM images of CDs-chitosan nanocomposite hydrogels in (Fig. 1 c, g, k and l) shows a layer with quasi-spherical structures and oriented wrinkles on the surface of the GA-CDs-CH, NB-CDs-CH and B-CDs-CH hydrogels [49], But the SEM images of the CD-free hydrogel showed an amorphous structure (Fig. l image l). Specifically, it is a proof of the stabilization and relatively uniform distribution of CDs in the hydrogel system.

The other optical properties of both CDs and CDs-chitosan nanocomposite hydrogels were also analyzed by fluorescence spectroscopy. The maximum fluorescence emissions of GA-CDs, NB-CD, and B-CDs are observed at 453, 421, and 430 nm, respectively with the excitation wavelengths of 360 nm. While CDs-CH hydrogel exhibited red shift at maximum fluorescence emission (Fig. 2 a, b and c) which may be ascribed to the hydrogen bonding among the CDs and chitosan causing to the widening of the particle size distribution [3].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of fluorescence sensing of microRNA-21 with nanocomposite hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: In summary, the protocol of the suggested assay for microRNA-21 detection shown in Scheme 1 (B). We synthesized three CDs-CH nanocomposite hydrogels which prevent aggregation of CDs and lead to enhancing their photo-stability. Increased photo-stability can be owing to the formation of CDs-CH–EDTA complex (Scheme 1B). CDs-CH nanocomposite hydrogels exhibited red shift fluorescence emission and may be owing to the highly 3D structure of hydrogels. As displayed in scheme 1B, the fluorescence intensity decreased with increasing of microRNA-21 concentrations. The specific interaction between DNA-probe and target microRNA-21 resulted in incomplete quenching of the fluorescence of the CDs-CH nanocomposite hydrogels (about 67%) (Scheme 1(B)).

Obviously, process of quenching of fluorescence is rooted in the interaction of a fluorophore with other species in solution, resulted in radiationless deactivation of the fluorophore to the ground state. Since there is no overlap between the absorption spectrum of microRNA-21 (λmax = 270 nm) [50] and the emission spectra of CDs samples. Therefore, the quenching mechanism cannot be due to the fluorescence resonance energy transfer and inner filter effect. According to the obtained results, the reason for quench the fluorescence intensity of CDs-CH nanocomposite hydrogels in the presence of target microRNA-21 can be owing to the transfer of energy from fluorophore to other molecules. Based on a previous study [51], the fluorescence quench of CDs may be caused by the surface quenching states of CDs. When the complementery target microRNA-21 is hybridized with DNA probe, they are placed on CDs inside the hydrogel, caused the aggregation of CDs as shown in Figure S1. Therefore, CDs are irregularly placed inside the hydrogel increasing the number of collisions and prevented the diffusion of light emitted in the hydrogel lead to quenching of the fluorescence signal.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the experimental conditions for microRNA-21 detection
ce:section.ce:para: The measuring of microRNA-21 with the proposed assay various effective parameters including different excitation wavelengths; concentration of NB-CDs; different pH at the range of 4–10; concentration of DNA probe and reaction time were optimized. The fluorescence spectra of the CDs-CH composite hydrogel recorded at different excitation wavelengths from 280 to 480 nm. The emission bands shifted from 400 to 530 nm with the increase of excitation wavelength from 280 to 480 nm (Fig. 2 a, b and c), the attractive features of excitation-dependent full-color emission provides CDs with bioimaging capability. The optimal excitation wavelengths were achieved 440, 360 and 360 nm for of GA-CDs-CH, NB-CDs-CH, B-CDs-CH nanocomposite hydrogels, respectively. The optimized concentration of NB-CDs utilized in this biosensor was obtained 0.1 mg/μL (Figure S2).

The stability of the GA-CDs-CH, NB CDs-CH, and B-CDs-CH nanocomposite hydrogel have been investigated. As shown in Figure S3 a-c, the fluorescence intensity was almost constant over a period of 60 min. As shown in Figure S4 a-c, the incubation times between CDs-CH nanocomposite hydrogels and target microRNA-21 in the range of 0–60 min was considered. The fluorescence intensity of CDs-CH nanocomposite hydrogels was quenched by the addition of target microRNA-21 in the first min and almost remained constant in the next 60 min. So, 10 min was selected as the optimized incubation time. The stability of the CDs was also investigated at different pH at the range of 4–10. As illustrated in Figure S5 (a-c), in acidic pH (pH < 7), the fluorescence of CDs was low. Because, in acidic condition amount of H+ in the medium is high so, the amine groups in chitosan are protonated (NH3 +), as a result, it cannot form an imine bond with aldehyde groups on CDs surfaces. There is no repulsion with the structure of EDTA and as a result it causes contraction and shrinkage of the hydrogel, which reduces the swelling of the hydrogel. At alkaline pH (pH > 7) owing to electrostatic interaction between carboxylate (COO−) groups in the structure of EDTA and Na+ in sodium hydroxide base, the amount of swelling and water absorption in the hydrogel decreases (Figure S5 a-c) [52]. The designed biosensor exhibited the best response in biological pH (7), thus, it was utilized as pH optimal for further measurement processes. The functionalization of DNA probes onto nanocomposite hydrogel surface was realized via imine bond formation among remaining –COH groups on CDs and –NH2 modified DNA probes. This conjugation was confirmed by fluorescence spectra. Owing to enhanced rigidity of the nanocomposite by functionalization with DNA probe, the fluorescence for DNA probe coated onto CDs-chitosan nanocomposite hydrogels was more than that of the CDs-chitosan nanocomposite hydrogels (Fig. S6a-c). As shown in Fig. 2(d–f), the effect of various amounts of DNA probes (1.0–10.0 nM) was examined for modification of CDs-chitosan nanocomposite hydrogels surface. The optimum value of the DNA probe was 2 nM for GA-CDs-CH and B-CDs-CH nanocomposite hydrogels and 1 nM for NB-CDs-CH nanocomposite hydrogels as revealed in Fig. 2(d–f). All data was obtain under stirring condition at 50 °C and acquired by taking the average of three replicates.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical efficiency and specificity of the biosensors
ce:section.ce:para: As presented in Fig. 3 a-c, fluorescence quenching can be considered upon the addition of target microRNA-21. The fluorescence signal changes (ΔF = F-Fo) as a function of the target microRNA −21 concentration is illustrated in Fig. 3(a–c), where F is the corresponding fluorescence intensity of the CD-CH nanocomposite hydrogel after addition of various concentrations of target microRNA-21 and Fo is the fluorescence intensity in the absence of target microRNA-21. In the optimum condition, the linear relation was achieved between decreased fluorescence intensity and logarithm of microRNA-21 concentration at 0.1 to 125 fM for GA-CDs-CH and NB-CDs-CH hydrogel and 0.1 to 26.3 fM for B-CDs-CH hydrogel. As can be seen in Table 1 , the dynamic range and limit of detection of present DNA hydrogel has been compared with other reported biosensors. The suggested biosensors have been one of the most selective and sensitive biosensors for microRNA-21 detection in comparison to another reported assays.

To explore the specificity of proposed biosensors, the fluorescence response of the probe toward the most serious interfering species including target microRNA-155 (T-155), microRNA-21 one base mismatched (M − 21) and microRNA-155 one base mismatched (M − 155) was recorded and they didn't any influence on microRNA-21 detection. Most importantly, as demonstrated in Fig. 4 (a–c) the biosensor had a good selectivity for hydrogels towards over other different sequence species. The experimental results revealed that the CDs-CH nanocomposite hydrogel performed much more selectively than the GA-CDs and NB-CDs (Figure S7 a,b).

To expression the performance of the proposed biosensors in biomedical applications, we used CDs-chitosan nanocomposite hydrogels to detect human breast cancer MCF-7 cell lines. The MCF-7 cell lines, with different cell numbers; 1000, 2000, 3000, 6000, 10,000, 20,000 and 25,000 (cells mL−1) was used as analyte. The obtained results indicated that with increased concentration of microRNA-21 in the cells of MCF-7 cell lines from 1000 to 25,000 cells mL−1, the values of ΔF = F0–F decrease (Fig. 3d–f). Moreover, the decrease of signal had a linear relation with the number of MCF-7 cell lines ranging from 1000 to 25,000 cells mL−1, with regression equation of ΔF = 141.52Ccell- 353.2 (R2 = 0.9883), ΔF = 113.94 Ccell+15.96 (R2 = 0.9958) for GA-CDs-CH, NB-CDs-CH nanocomposite hydrogel, respectively, and the detection limit was calculated to be 310, 364 and 552 cells mL−1 for GA-CDs-CH, NB-CDs-CH and B-CDs-CH nanocomposite hydrogel, respectively. Linear range was obtained in the range of 1000–6000 cells mL−1 with regression equation of ΔF = 140.75 Ccell-397.04 (R2 = 0.9776) for B-CDs-CH nanocomposite hydrogel.

The MTT assay was utilized to estimate the cytotoxicity of the CDs against MCF-7 cells. To accomplish this test, MCF-7 cells (2500 cells per mL) were seeded in a 96-well plate and placed in a 37 °C incubator for 48 h to permit attachment. Then, MCF-7 cells were treated with various amounts of CDs (0.1, 0.2, 0.4 and 0.8 mg mL−1) in DMEM in the presence of 5% CO2 and 95% relative humidity for 24 h and untreated cells were utilized as controls. After that, the supernatant was altered with 200 μL of fresh medium containing 20 μL of MTT solution and kept 3 h at 37 °C. Then the supernatant was discarded and 100 μL of DMSO was added to each well to solubilize MTT. Finally, a microplate reader was applied to measure the absorbance values of each well at a wavelength of 545/630 nm. Each measurement was repeated 5 times. 95% of the cells survived by adding 0.4 mg mL−1 of different GA-CDs, NB-CDs and B-CDs (Figure S5 a-c). Because the functionalized CDs were negligible cytotoxicity toward MCF-7 cells, they could be used as labels for cell imaging applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Imaging of miR-21 in MCF-7 cells
ce:section.ce:para: The potential of the synthesized CDs was demonstrated by the MCF-7 cells imaging study. MCF-7 cells were seeded in 6-well culture with a density of 7000 cells per well and placed in an incubator containing 5% CO2 at 37 °C for 24 h. Then, Fresh culture medium containing NB-CDs (0.4 mg mL−1) was added to the cells and incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. Finally, the cells were washed with phosphate buffer (PBS, 1x) 5 times. Then add 1 mL of 4% formaldehyde and leave it at room temperature for 4 min to fix the cells. After that, remove the formaldehyde and add 1 mL of phosphate buffer (PBS, 1x) to each well. Multicolor imaging of cells was achieved simultaneously from the blue channel, green channel, yellow channel and red channel for microRNA detection. As can be seen, the MCF-7 cells treated with CDs showed no change in shape. Fig. 5 (A-L) indicated that the photoluminescence is observed at cytoplasmic area. The MCF-7 cells homogeneously and successfully labeled by CDs and conserved their fluorescent characteristics in the cellular environment.

In summary, we synthesized CDs-chitosan nanocomposite hydrogels by reacting aldehyde-containing CDs and chitosan, then by forming an imine bond between the amine in chitosan and aldehyde on the CDs surface and utilized for rapid, selective and sensitive detection of microRNA-21 at fM concentration range and also for MCF-7 cancer cells analysis. Also, it was demonstrated that the proposed hydrogel had good mechanical, physical and fluorescence properties. Indeed, CDs as fluorophore and the gelator play a significant role in the hydrogel structure. The benefit of using the hydrogel method is that prevents aggregation because the CDs are placed in the hydrogel network in a regular and dispersed manner. Compared to CDs, the emission band of CDs-CH hydrogel shifted to the red areas. It seems that the designed hydrogel can be utilized for drug release, and more biological applications and the proposed strategy promised for prepare and development of other hydrogel based fluorophores. Furthermore, since hydrogels exhibited multicolor fluorescence, high mechanical strength, high adhesiveness and strong antibacterial properties, they are good for sensing and biosensing platforms. Furthermore, incorporation of functionalized nanomaterials within hydrogel provide a 3D nanocomposite with facilitated sensing property for biomarker detection.

Susan Mohammadi; doing the experiment, writing and data analysis, Somayeh Mohammadi; synthesis and characterization of materials, real sample analysis and bioimaging; Abdollah Salimi supervision, reviewing and Editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge Iran National Science Foundation (INSF) ( grant number 96002684) Research Office of University of Kurdistan (grant number 4.1404086) for the financial support. The Iranian Nanotechnology Initiative support is also acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2020.121895.
